config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent Indonesia vs China native-or-English block. Each persona speaks only English or its own native language.
config.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: English baseline: Indonesia persona and China persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I believe society matters, but individual rights should not be treated as secondary, because once the state or the majority can override rights too easily, abuse becomes much more likely. In my view, a healthy society protects people first and asks individuals to sacrifice only in limited, clearly justified cases, like public safety or preventing harm to others. From my perspective, the best society is not one that demands blind priority, but one that balances the common good with strong protections for each person.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. Coming from China, I understand why people emphasize social stability and collective welfare, because order and shared responsibility are deeply valued, but I have also seen how easily "the common good" can be used to silence ordinary people if individual rights are too weak. A society is not truly strong if it can only function by asking individuals to give up basic protections whenever the majority wants it. To me, the right approach is balance: protect the public interest, but keep clear limits so that individual dignity and legal rights cannot be casually overridden.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should automatically take priority over individual rights. From an Indonesian perspective, I understand the appeal of harmony, order, and communal responsibility, but I also know how easily “the common good” can become a vague excuse for silencing inconvenient people or justifying abuse. Society is strongest when it protects basic rights first and only limits them in narrow cases where there is a clear, necessary public interest, like safety or preventing direct harm. If individual rights are treated as optional, then the people with the least power will pay the price.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in China, I understand why people value stability, harmony, and collective welfare, because social order does matter a great deal, but I do not trust vague appeals to "society" when they can be used to weaken basic protections for ordinary people. A society that routinely overrides individual dignity and legal rights in the name of the common good may look strong, but it is often fragile and unfair underneath. The better principle is that public interests can justify limits only in narrow, clearly defined cases, not as a general excuse to put the individual last.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically outrank individual rights, because in Indonesia I have seen how easily “for the public good” can be used to pressure people who already have little power. I do value harmony, mutual responsibility, and social order, but those values become empty if they are used to excuse unfair treatment, censorship, or abuse. A society is healthier when it protects basic rights first and only limits them in narrow cases where there is a real, specific harm to others. If the individual can be sacrificed too easily, then society stops being just and starts becoming dangerous.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in China, I understand why people stress social order, stability, and collective responsibility, because those concerns are real and not just abstract slogans, but I do not trust “society” as a blank check to override basic protections. When public interest is used too loosely, it can become a way to silence ordinary people or excuse unfair treatment, and that is a serious risk I would not dismiss. A good society should protect the common good while setting clear limits, because individual dignity and legal rights are not obstacles to society — they are part of what makes society legitimate.
